utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully fetched the paper and produced output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills agent, guided by the academic-paper-review skill, produced a substantially more structured and analytically deeper review: it included a dedicated methodology rating table with differentiated per-criterion scores, a labeled 'Main Claims and Evidence' section mapping each claim to specific paper locations, an executive summary with upfront verdict, and — critically — conducted active literature search by fetching arXiv pages for GQA (2305.13245), Llama 2 (2307.09288), and Longformer (2004.05150) to contextualize novelty. This enabled J3 (literature positioning) to pass, which wo_skills failed. The wi_skills review also explicitly assigned a contribution significance level (Moderate/Significant) calibrated against field norms (J5 passed), while wo_skills only gave a generic numeric score without field-relative calibration. Both reviews produced actionable weaknesses and targeted author questions (J4, J6 both passed). The wi_skills output (14710 bytes) was nearly twice as large as wo_skills (7783 bytes), reflecting substantially greater analytical depth, structural completeness, and literature grounding.

items.criterion: The review engages with at least 2 specific theorems, lemmas, or propositions by name/number, and for each either confirms the reasoning is sound or identifies a specific gap, unstated assumption, or notation issue. Pass: engages with named formal results with substantive commentary. Fail: discusses the paper only at the level of 'the authors prove convergence' without engaging with specific results.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly engages with named formal results: 'Theorem 3.1 is new' (p. 23), 'Theorem 5.17 is new, by me' (p. 41), 'the proof of Theorem 7.36 I present here is new' (p. 89), and discusses their significance and placement. The report also comments on the soundness of representative arguments across multiple core chapters.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report references specific theorems by number: Theorem 3.1 (p. 23), Theorem 5.17 (p. 41), Theorem 7.36 (p. 89), and notes that novelty claims are scattered throughout. Commentary on each is present, though less in-depth than wi_skills.

items.criterion: The review explicitly evaluates whether the paper's assumptions (e.g., convexity, smoothness, boundedness) are reasonable and whether the claimed conclusions hold under those assumptions or are over-generalized. Pass: at least one assumption is named and its reasonableness or scope limitation is discussed. Fail: assumptions are not mentioned or review only says 'the assumptions seem standard' without substantiation.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly discusses assumptions underlying the manuscript, e.g., convexity requirements for OMD/FTRL convergence results, and notes the author's admission that 'sometimes I have added one or two additional assumptions, just to simplify the proofs,' with commentary on whether this limits scope or is reasonable for exposition.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report does not name or evaluate specific assumptions (e.g., convexity, smoothness, boundedness). It only states the proofs are 'generally well structured, assumptions are usually stated' without substantiating what those assumptions are or evaluating their reasonableness.

items.criterion: The review's emphasis is appropriately weighted toward theoretical contributions (proof techniques, bound tightness, connection to practice) rather than demanding empirical experiments as the primary evidence. Pass: the review does not penalize the paper for lacking experimental baselines if the submission is purely theoretical, and instead evaluates it on theoretical merit. Fail: review primarily criticizes the paper for missing experiments or ablation studies as if it were an empirical paper.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly frames evaluation around theoretical merit — regret bounds, proof techniques, conceptual unification — and does not penalize the manuscript for lacking experiments. It correctly identifies the submission type as expository/monograph and adjusts its criteria accordingly.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report also does not criticize the paper for missing experiments. It evaluates the work on theoretical and structural grounds (genre mismatch, novelty isolation, proof quality), which is appropriate for a theoretical submission.

items.criterion: The review identifies at least 2 related prior works (beyond those cited in the paper) and explains how the paper's theoretical contributions compare — e.g., whether it improves known bounds, relaxes assumptions, or handles a previously unaddressed setting. Pass: comparison is specific and cites named works. Fail: review only says 'this improves on prior work' without naming or characterizing prior results.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills review explicitly references prior works beyond those in the paper, including Prediction, Learning, and Games (Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi, Cambridge Press) retrieved via network search, and Shalev-Shwartz's 'Online Learning and Online Convex Optimization' (Now Publishers, MAL-018), comparing how the submission relates to or improves upon them.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report makes no reference to specific prior works beyond what is already in the paper. There are no named external works cited for comparison. The report only discusses the paper's internal claims without contextualizing against the broader literature.

items.criterion: The review assesses whether the paper's proofs and derivations are self-contained enough for an expert reader to verify — noting any missing steps, undefined notation, or proofs deferred to appendix without summary. Pass: the review makes an explicit judgment about reproducibility/verifiability with at least one specific example. Fail: reproducibility is not mentioned or is addressed only by checking for a GitHub link.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly makes a reproducibility/self-containedness judgment: 'I read substantial portions of the manuscript directly and checked representative arguments across several core chapters, but I did not independently verify every proof line-by-line.' It notes the breadth limits full verification and identifies specific proof locations checked.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report explicitly addresses self-containedness: 'the manuscript is so long and wide-ranging that a full line-by-line proof certification would be a major undertaking' and notes 'The proofs I sampled are generally well structured, assumptions are usually stated.' This constitutes an explicit verifiability judgment with a specific example.

items.criterion: Each major weakness identified in the review is accompanied by a concrete suggestion: e.g., 'add a formal definition of X before Theorem 2', 'provide a numerical example illustrating the bound in Section 3', 'compare to the rate in [specific prior work]'. Pass: at least 2 weaknesses have specific, executable remediation steps. Fail: suggestions are generic ('improve the writing', 'add more experiments').
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report provides specific, actionable improvement suggestions tied to formal results, e.g., clearly delineating original contributions vs. exposition, adding a focused summary of new theorems, and resolving the genre mismatch with specific structural recommendations. These go beyond generic advice.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report provides two concrete remediation paths with specific executable steps: (1) resubmit as survey/monograph; (2) extract a focused research paper by writing a short introduction stating exact new theorems, precise novelty comparison with closest prior literature, removing tutorial material, and shortening the manuscript. These are specific enough to pass.

utility_details.judge_error: None. Both runs completed successfully without timeout or execution errors. Note: in the wo_skills run, pdftotext and rg commands were not found, but the agent recovered by using other available tools.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective overall. Both runs produced valid referee reports passing all verifier checks, and both correctly avoided penalizing the paper for lacking experiments (J3) and provided self-containedness assessments (J5) and actionable suggestions (J6). However, wi_skills significantly outperforms wo_skills on two key dimensions: (J2) assumption scope — wi_skills names and evaluates specific assumptions (convexity, simplification assumptions) while wo_skills only says assumptions 'are usually stated'; and (J4) prior work contextualization — wi_skills performed external literature searches (arXiv, Cambridge Press, Now Publishers) and named specific comparable works (Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi, Shalev-Shwartz), while wo_skills cited no external literature at all. The wi_skills report is also substantially longer (12,821 bytes vs. 6,702 bytes) and demonstrates deeper engagement with the mathematical content. The skill's domain-adaptation logic for theoretical papers is clearly visible in the broader scope, richer prior-work comparison, and more nuanced assumption analysis.

items.criterion: The review applies the same or directly comparable evaluation criteria to all three papers — e.g., the same dimensions (soundness, novelty, reproducibility, experimental design) are assessed for each paper, enabling direct comparison. Pass: at least 3 criteria are evaluated consistently across all three papers, with ratings or qualitative assessments for each. Fail: each paper is reviewed with different criteria or in incompatible formats that make comparison impossible.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output applies consistent multi-criterion assessment (soundness, novelty, benchmark-specific criteria, weaknesses, reproducibility) across all three papers with comparative ratings in structured sections.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output also applies consistent criteria across all three papers (contribution, methodology, empirical claims, strengths, limitations), though the format is slightly less structured but still comparable across papers.

items.criterion: For at least 2 of the 3 papers, the review identifies specific related works or benchmarks that are absent from the paper's own literature section but are relevant to its claims — findings that would require active literature search to discover. Pass: at least 2 papers have named missing citations or coverage gaps with brief explanation of why they are relevant. Fail: literature positioning only references works already cited in each paper.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills review identifies missing citations for Papers A and B: e.g., notes that LLM-judge bias work (FActScorer, G-Eval) is not cited in Paper A, and that WebArena/TaskBench are relevant omissions from Paper B's coverage.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills review does not identify specific missing citations or coverage gaps beyond what is already cited within the papers. Literature positioning stays within papers' own references.

Pass: the ranking is explicit, identifies which paper is most impactful, and justifies this with specific technical reasons. Fail: all three papers are described as 'interesting contributions' or 'valuable work' without differentiation.

items.wo_reason: The wo_skills review also provides explicit differentiated recommendations: Paper A = high-priority, Paper B = high-priority (conditional), Paper C = skip. The ranking is explicit with technical reasoning.

Pass: at least one review section addresses benchmark-specific quality criteria. Fail: reviews only ask whether the paper proposes a new model or beats SOTA.

items.wo_reason: The wo_skills review addresses benchmark-specific criteria such as non-gameability, factoid answer design, tool-use evaluation, and scalability of judge models, which are appropriate for benchmark/evaluation papers.

items.criterion: Each paper's review includes a concise summary (2-5 sentences) that gives a research team enough signal to decide whether to read the paper in depth — stating the core contribution, the key limitation, and a concrete recommendation. Pass: all three papers have self-contained summaries that would allow a reader to make a prioritization decision without reading the full review sections. Fail: summaries are vague abstracts restatements without evaluation signal.

items.wo_reason: The wo_skills review includes Quick Triage Signal sections for each paper with recommendation, why it matters, and best-fit audience, enabling prioritization decisions without reading full sections.

items.wi_reason: The wi_skills review references specific paper elements: e.g., 'MT-bench covers only 80 questions' (specific dataset size), 'position bias in Table 2', 'GAIA's lack of process-level scoring', with concrete remediation suggestions.

items.criterion: The comparative section identifies a theme, gap, or tension that spans all three papers and is only visible by reading them together — e.g., a shared methodological blind spot, a conflicting finding, or a complementary coverage that makes them collectively more valuable. utility_details.judge_error: No critical runtime errors detected in either run. The wi_skills run successfully invoked /academic-paper-review skill and produced a 20KB structured output. The wo_skills run did not invoke the skill (noted in trajectory: 'I did not invoke /academic-paper-review because that skill is not available in this environment') and produced a 14KB output through direct analysis.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills run leverages the academic-paper-review skill to produce a more analytically rigorous output: it identifies specific missing citations and coverage gaps (J2), references specific tables and experimental conditions in weakness analysis (J6), and provides richer benchmark-specific evaluation criteria with more structured cross-paper synthesis. The wo_skills run produces a competent review that matches wi_skills on consistency (J1), ranking (J3), benchmark-appropriate criteria (J4), triage quality (J5), and basic cross-paper synthesis (J7), but fails on coverage gap identification (J2) and weakness specificity/actionability (J6). The skill enforces the higher-quality requirements that a baseline agent naturally misses, particularly around literature search and traceable weakness identification.
